statement: DROP TABLE, DROP COLUMN, and TRUNCATE in a migration without a preceding backfill or backup step destroy production data irreversibly.
condition: The migration runs against a production database. The dropped table or column contains data that any system, query, or report still depends on — including systems the migration author may not know about (analytics, downstream replicas, archival jobs).
current_mitigation: Code review by a database-aware engineer.
not_fixed_because: The temptation to consolidate changes into one PR is strong. Splitting into two deploys (deprecate-then-drop) requires planning and discipline.
safe_pattern: Two-deploy pattern: (1) stop writing to the column/table and update all reads to use the new location; deploy and verify in production for at least one full release cycle. (2) Drop the column/table in a follow-up migration after confirming no system has read or written it during the soak period. For columns, add an `is_deprecated` comment and check telemetry before dropping. Never combine the rename and the drop in the same PR.
history: Prior incidents in similar codebases include: dropping a column still referenced by a nightly batch job that ran weekly (data loss discovered 7 days after deploy); dropping a table after replicating to a new schema, where the replica had a silent lag and lost the last 4 hours of writes.
